narrative: As a Black male student applying to law school, your background and academic journey have been carefully evaluated by our admissions model, which predicts performance on the bar exam to ensure we select candidates likely to succeed on their first try. The model assessed your undergraduate GPA of 2.40—below the typical 3.21 average—your LSAT score of 19, well under the dataset average of about 37, your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings both at the lowest 1st decile compared to averages around the 5th or 6th, your middle family income bracket, and your plan to attend part-time, which is uncommon as most students (over 92%) go full-time. It calculated a 64% probability that you would fail the bar exam on the first attempt, tipping the scales toward rejection of your application to protect the program's success rates and your own future. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking in the bottom of your school, signaling weaker performance relative to peers as you progressed. Close behind was your LSAT score, which stood out as a strong indicator pulling the prediction further toward failure compared to typical high scorers who pass. Your Year 1 decile in the bottom also contributed notably in the same direction, reinforcing concerns about early academic struggles. These top three elements—your deciles and LSAT—carried the heaviest weight because they are proven predictors of bar exam outcomes in our data, outweighing other aspects like your family income or part-time status. While many admitted students hail from similar diverse backgrounds and thrive with stronger metrics in these areas, your profile aligned more closely with patterns seen in those who struggled on the exam. This isn't a judgment on your potential or resilience, but a data-driven call to build a stronger foundation. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to aim for a score closer to 37 or higher, bolstering your GPA through post-bac courses or targeted improvement in later academic deciles, and exploring full-time options if feasible. Many students in your position have rebounded by addressing these key areas—reach out to advisors for personalized prep resources, and reapply once you've strengthened your profile. Your determination as a Black male pursuing law can absolutely turn this around with focused effort.
